preFix.title: Sausage and egg pie
preFix.subtitle: A shortcrust pastry pie with sausage meat, hard-boiled eggs, and herbs
preFix.excerpt: Sausage and egg pie is a British picnic and lunchbox staple. A deep shortcrust case filled with seasoned sausage meat wrapped around whole hard-boiled eggs, baked until the pastry is golden. It eats well warm or cold and travels well in a tin.
preFix.sourceNotes: British savoury pie tradition. Sausage meat and egg pies appear in British cookbooks from the 18th century onwards as practical cold-keeping food. The form with whole eggs set into the sausage meat is a classic British picnic and cold-buffet item.

content.text: Sausage and egg pie is the kind of British food that works better the day after it is made. The pastry firms up, the sausage meat slices cleanly, and the whole egg set into the filling gives each slice a clear cross-section. Use a deep tin so the filling has enough room. Good pork sausage meat makes a real difference: the pre-seasoned kind found at a butcher works better than squeezing sausages.

items.name: Eggs
items.prepNote: 4 hard-boiled, peeled; 1 beaten for glaze; 1 raw for the filling

content.text: Boil 4 eggs for 9 minutes. Cool in cold water, then peel and set aside.
Mix the sausage meat with the grated onion, sage, 1 raw egg, salt, and pepper until well combined.
Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Line a deep rectangular or round tin with two-thirds of the pastry, letting it hang over the rim.
Press half the sausage meat evenly into the base. Lay the 4 hard-boiled eggs in a line down the centre. Press the remaining sausage meat over and around them, encasing the eggs completely.
Roll out the remaining pastry and lay it over the top. Press the edges to seal and trim. Brush with beaten egg. Make a few small cuts in the lid to let steam escape. Chill for 30 minutes.
Bake for 50 to 55 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the filling is cooked through. Rest for 15 minutes before slicing. Eat warm or cold.

items.fix: Check at 50 minutes by pressing the top: it should feel firm, not hard. A little fat in the sausage meat keeps it moist.
items.cause: Overbaked, or the sausage meat was very lean.
items.symptom: The sausage meat is dry inside

items.fix: Place the tin directly on the lowest oven shelf to give the base direct heat.
items.cause: Deep tin insulated the base from the oven heat.

items.fix: Let the pie cool for at least 15 minutes before slicing, and use a sharp knife.
items.cause: Pie cut while still too hot.
items.symptom: The eggs are impossible to slice through cleanly

content.text: Sausage and egg pie belongs to the British tradition of cold-eating food. It is made the day before and served at a picnic, a cold buffet, or cut into for lunch with no reheating. It is close to the Scotch egg in spirit, but the pie format allows more of both egg and sausage meat and feeds more people from a single tin.
